name: Olorun
description: Olorun, also known as Olodumare, stands as the supreme being in the Yoruba religion of West Africa, specifically Nigeria and Benin. Olorun is considered the ultimate source of all existence, possessing immense power and wisdom. While often described as a singular, all-powerful entity, Olorun is also understood as embodying the concept of the divine in its totality. Olorun is not typically directly worshipped through shrines or sacrifices, as the deity is seen as being beyond the immediate reach of humans. Instead, worship is directed toward the Orishas, the divine emissaries who act as intermediaries between Olorun and humanity. Olorun's influence permeates all aspects of Yoruba life, from the natural world to social structures, and is often invoked in prayers and ceremonies. The concept of ase, the divine life force, is intrinsically linked to Olorun, as the deity is the source of this vital energy that animates all things. Olorun's nature is abstract and profound, representing the ultimate mystery of creation and the divine order.
subtitle: The Supreme Being

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Olorun tasked Obatala with creating the earth, but Obatala became intoxicated with palm wine and failed in his duty. Oduduwa then took over, completing the task by scattering earth upon the waters from a snail shell, and releasing a hen to spread the earth, thus creating land. This myth illustrates the importance of responsibility and the consequences of failure in the face of divine mandates, while also highlighting the collaborative nature of creation.
keyMyths.source: Idowu, E. Bolaji. *Olódùmarè: God in Yoruba Belief*. Longman, 1962.

keyMyths.title: The Giving of Ase
keyMyths.description: Olorun bestowed Ase, the divine power to make things happen, upon the Orishas and humanity. This gift allowed them to influence the world and fulfill their destinies. The amount of Ase granted varied among the Orishas, determining their individual powers and responsibilities. Understanding and harnessing Ase is central to Yoruba spirituality, as it empowers individuals to manifest their desires and navigate the complexities of life, connecting them directly to the divine.
keyMyths.source: Awolalu, J. Omosade. *Yoruba Beliefs and Sacrificial Rites*. Longman, 1979.

keyMyths.title: Olorun and the Orishas
keyMyths.description: While Olorun is the supreme being, the Orishas are the intermediaries who interact with the human world. Each Orisha embodies specific aspects of nature and human experience, such as love, justice, and healing. They act as messengers and representatives of Olorun, carrying out the divine will and providing guidance to humanity. This hierarchical structure allows for a more accessible and personalized form of worship, while still acknowledging the ultimate authority of Olorun.
keyMyths.source: Abimbola, Wande. *Ifá: An Exposition of Ifá Literary Corpus*. Ibadan University Press, 1976.

sources.author: Idowu, E. Bolaji
sources.description: A comprehensive study of the concept of God in Yoruba religion, focusing on Olorun/Olodumare.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: Represents Olorun's omnipresence and ability to permeate all things.

associations.name: Creation
associations.significance: Olorun is the ultimate creator, responsible for bringing the universe and all life into existence.

associations.name: Destiny
associations.significance: Olorun holds the power to determine the fate of individuals and the course of events.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Symbolizes Olorun's singularity and supreme status as the ultimate being.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Represents Olorun's radiant power and the source of life and energy.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: Symbolizes purity, holiness, and Olorun's transcendence.

associations.name: Wisdom
associations.significance: Olorun possesses infinite knowledge and understanding of the universe.

worshipPractices.name: Prayer
worshipPractices.description: Devotees offer prayers to Olorun, seeking guidance, blessings, and intervention in their lives. Prayers are often accompanied by offerings of kola nuts and other symbolic items.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings are made to Olorun as a sign of respect and gratitude. These offerings can include kola nuts, water, and other symbolic items. The act of offering is a way of connecting with the divine and seeking blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Divination
worshipPractices.description: While Olorun is not directly involved in divination, the Ifá divination system is used to interpret Olorun's will and guidance. The verses of Ifá are believed to contain the wisdom and knowledge of Olorun, which can be accessed through divination.

festivals.name: Odun Olojo
festivals.description: A significant festival in Ife, Nigeria, dedicated to the Yoruba deity Ogun, but implicitly acknowledges Olorun as the supreme being from whom Ogun derives his power. It involves rituals and ceremonies to honor the ancestors and seek blessings for the community.

festivals.name: New Yam Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the harvest of new yams, a staple food in Yoruba culture. While not directly dedicated to Olorun, the festival involves giving thanks for the bounty of the earth, which is ultimately attributed to Olorun's creation and blessings.

cultural.modernLegacy: Olorun remains a central figure in Yoruba religious traditions practiced worldwide. His influence extends to various syncretic religions in the Americas, such as Santería and Candomblé, where he is revered as the supreme creator. Olorun's name and attributes continue to inspire art, music, and literature, reflecting his enduring significance in African and diasporic cultures.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Yoruba cosmology, Olorun's existence transcends the beginning of time, existing before all other deities and entities. The origin of Olorun is not typically described in terms of birth or creation, as Olorun is considered self-existent and the ultimate source of all being. Olorun brought forth the universe and all its components, including the Orishas, who serve as intermediaries between the divine and humanity. The creation narrative often involves Olorun entrusting the Orishas with specific tasks and responsibilities in shaping and governing the world. Olorun's essence is seen as permeating all of creation, embodying the divine in its totality. While often distant from direct involvement in human affairs, Olorun's power and influence are ever-present, shaping the destinies of individuals and the course of history. The concept of Olorun as the supreme being is fundamental to Yoruba religious beliefs and practices, providing a framework for understanding the relationship between the divine and the mortal realm. The name Olodumare is also used to refer to the same supreme being, often interchangeably with Olorun.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Olorun is not the central character in many Yoruba myths, his role as the supreme creator and source of power is often implied or referenced. Many stories focus on the Orishas and their interactions with humanity, but these interactions are ultimately understood to be within the framework established by Olorun. One notable story involves Olorun sending Obatala, the Orisha of creation, to mold the earth from a primordial swamp. Obatala, however, became intoxicated and created imperfect beings, leading to the existence of disabilities and deformities in the world. This story highlights Olorun's ultimate authority and the limitations of even the most powerful Orishas. Another myth tells of Olorun's decision to withdraw from direct interaction with humanity due to their constant complaints and dissatisfaction. This withdrawal led to the need for intermediaries, the Orishas, to act as messengers and mediators between Olorun and humankind. These myths underscore Olorun's transcendence and the importance of maintaining a respectful relationship with the divine.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of Olorun is often indirect, as he is considered too transcendent to be approached directly by humans. Instead, devotees typically focus their worship on the Orishas, who serve as intermediaries between Olorun and the mortal realm. However, Olorun is acknowledged in prayers and offerings, and his name is invoked in various rituals and ceremonies. Temples dedicated solely to Olorun are rare, but shrines and altars may include symbols and representations that honor him as the supreme being. Kola nuts are often used as offerings to Olorun, as they are considered sacred and symbolic of life and prosperity. The act of offering kola nuts is a way of expressing gratitude and seeking blessings from the divine. Divination practices, such as Ifá, are also used to interpret Olorun's will and guidance. The verses of Ifá are believed to contain the wisdom and knowledge of Olorun, which can be accessed through divination practitioners.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Olorun's legacy extends far beyond the borders of West Africa, influencing religious traditions and cultural practices in the African diaspora. In syncretic religions such as Santería and Candomblé, which emerged in the Americas during the transatlantic slave trade, Olorun is revered as the supreme creator and source of all power. These religions blended Yoruba beliefs with elements of Catholicism and other indigenous traditions, creating unique expressions of African spirituality. Olorun's name and attributes continue to inspire art, music, and literature in both Africa and the diaspora. His enduring significance reflects the resilience of African cultural heritage and the ongoing quest for spiritual connection. The concept of a supreme, all-powerful being resonates with people of diverse backgrounds, transcending cultural and geographical boundaries. Olorun's legacy serves as a testament to the enduring power of faith and the human capacity for spiritual expression.

shortDescription: Olorun, the supreme creator in Yoruba religion, embodies the divine in its totality. Revered as the ultimate source of existence and wisdom.
